sentences.text: [TONE]
Well, Judenrat was the organization [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that was like a self-government in the ghetto.
But it never worked out as such, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was too-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] too much influenced by constantly changing decrees.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] now, the head of the Judenrat was Czerniakow.
Was very prominent figure in Jewish community before the war.
Very decent man.

And that was the problem, because he-- he had the courage to resist some-- some rules and regulation, but nevertheless, some were like this Jewish police force was established, which was a department of the Judenrat.
I don't think so.
I don't think so.
I didn't have any personal contact.
I know that they were deciding, or probably trying to influence the delivery and amount of food to the ghetto.
Medical help, if there was any available, for the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- probably the schools, or some organization, and I suppose that the self-help that Jews organized, that was on behalf of this Judenrat.
Because for the time, in the beginning, in '41, there were some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] helping the poors.
There were kitchen soups.
There were some orphanages.
There were some schools.
And that was with help and on behalf of this Judenrat.
How we were fed?
We had-- everybody was getting the coupons, food coupons.
That was a minimal.
It was almost a starvation diet.
Not enough.
We were still able to have some valuable things, and we were selling this.
That was my mother, mainly some jewelry.
We were selling this and trying to buy, on the black market, some foodstuffs.
Bread, sometimes meat, sugar, whatever.
Because there was some black market going on over the walls or under the walls.
And that was look at this.
I mean, it-- it was tolerated, let's say.
So we were able to eat proper-- eat-- I mean, we were not starving.
Sometimes we were hungry.
We would-- that wasn't enough.
But by no means, we were not starving.
That was very painful, was very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was hard to accept.
Because they were not only strangers that were starving, but they were people in my own family.
I remember I saw once a cousin.
Her husband was a dentist.
He died, and she was left with four little children.
And I saw this woman begging on the street, walking from one small store to another, begging for food.
That was in my own family.
The cousins were poor.
We were trying to help as much as possible.
But after a while, you get immune to it, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's hard.
It's very hard now to justify, but you want to survive.
Under the circumstances, it's survival.
That was-- that-- that was the idea.
It was painful.
You saw people dying in the ghetto.
You-- you saw your own relatives being hungry.
And still, you can-- within your means, you can help only so and so much.
I don't-- maybe ourself.
Because later, when we were working outside of the ghetto, we were smuggling.
We were bringing food.
We were exchanging our clothes or stuff of our friends.
Because we had this chance.
We were working on the other side of the ghetto and coming back home.
We had a chance to buy-- to bring food to the ghetto.
So I can consider myself a smuggler, and I don't mind.
You know, I think I am losing, a little bit, this chronological order.
Is that OK?
Shall I answer your question?
Yes.
We got-- that was later in the-- in-- during our life in the ghetto.
So they were working, labor camp, established outside.
They were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were repair shop for the Luftwaffe, which was the German Air Force.
They-- parts to the plane or the trucks or cars or whatever.
And that was outskirts of Warsaw, and they needed people to work there.
So a few hundred people were needed for this job.
And Henry was able to-- to get this job over there.
And also, he tried to get me to it, because that was a break at that time.
First of all, the working conditions, you-- you work, you were able to be outdoors.
That was some grass and trees, and you saw the sky.
And-- and-- and the work wasn't too hard.
I was working in the kitchen, cooking.
Peeling these rotten potatoes and preparing soup for the prisoners, for the-- for these working people.
And also cleaning the offices which were nearby.
But we still had the time to enjoy the normal things in life, which is fresh air and sun and whatever.
So that was a great opportunity.

And also, on the way home through the ghetto, we were able to buy-- to-- to-- to get the foodstuff which was given to our families and-- and to other people, or we were exchanging-- I mean, we were taking stuff for friends and bringing them back-- back food, whatever they needed to have there.
Yes.
Yes, that was closer towards the end of the ghetto.
In the beginning, there were not-- yes, so that was '41, which seems like that life will be tolerable.
Like I said, they were this-- this Judenrat, probably, that organized this self-help, and also encouraged people, whoever it was, in a position to help others.
So there were even-- there were sort of parties that people would gather together and collect money for the poor, collect money for especially sick family, or a sick child, or whatever.
So that was like a [INAUDIBLE] of a normal life, but was still livable.
And I mentioned there were [INAUDIBLE].
That's-- that was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they-- they-- they were teaching different trades.
And I took the sewing and millinery course.
There were teachers, dressmakers, that gave us the instruction.
And so I was occupied with this, and then that was rather-- rather it made sense, let's say, and you thought that there would be a future.
And Henry was taking different jobs, I mean, working for the Germans.
They were-- I am calling job, but I-- that was slave labor, because that was never paid for.
A bowl of soup, or maybe some piece of meat or a little sugar.
So that was the-- the-- the highest reward, what it was.
But that was nothing on permanent basis.

At times, he even started to-- to produce something that was like this little cooking utensil, like a small oven, which required very small amount of wood or coal, and it would heat up quickly, and you could cook on it quickly.
So that was a very good idea, but unfortunately, didn't last too long.
Because again, there came a new changed border of the ghetto, and we had to leave everything and move to completely different quarters.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then-- let me tell you some pictures from the ghetto.
That-- there-- there was a question that we ate and other people were starving.
That was with us all the time.
And when I think about it now, you know, I am looking for-- for justification of it.
So the conditions, the ghetto, the streets were immensely crowded.
Because there were homeless people, sick people quickly deteriorating.
That was typhoid.
It was decimating people.
It was very easy to-- to catch, because it was carried by the lice.
And being in this crowd of dirty people, when you walk by, you could have gotten infected lice, and that was the typhoid.
So that was the street.
The worst picture were the children, because the parents, quite often they-- they were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] killed, probably, or-- or [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taken to work, taken somewhere else.
And they were like little animals.
I remember a situation that the smugglers, after they probably finished their business, they ate and drank and got sick in front of this tavern, and they would vomit.
And within a few minutes, the beggars, the little children, ran to it and ate it up.
They clean up the street.
So that was the complete dehumanization.
But you saw it on every corner, on every step.
There were people dying on the street.
People, they were begging, beggars.
They became some professional beggars.
I don't blame them.
But it was tremendously disturbing at that time, because some had this system, this monotony, for hours.
The same, the same thing, you know?
And it was just so hard to-- to-- to tolerate, so you got accustomed to it.
You got accustomed to it, and they-- it-- it-- it was just like a normal-- a normal way of life.
A normal thing.
The other hand, I remember this little girl was coming to the house every-- around dinnertime.
And she was too shy to knock at the door, so she would sing.
Every night, the same song.
And she was coming with a little pot.
And she was always was getting the-- some soup and whatever we had for dinner.
And I saw this child, how she was losing weight from week to week.
Her voice, her little voice was weaker and weaker.
And one day, she stopped.
She didn't come anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had-- in Warsaw, there was a beggar, crazy man.
His name was Rubinstein.
He was running from one yard to another, begging people and screaming, 'We are all equal.
We are [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am dying now.
You will die tomorrow.' So that was this memento.
He was right.
And that was a part of our life.
When you walk through the street of ghetto, you saw there were some people that were still decently dressed and fed.
But then you saw a group of-- of children that would grab your purse, or when you were carrying a loaf of bread or something.
It happened once to us.
We got our-- our quota for the day.
That was a loaf of bread and maybe pound of marmalade, and we-- we were walking.
And this fellow came to us, grabbed it, and ran away.
You know, your first reaction, you are mad.
Angry.
Henry ran after him.
But luckily, you know, he didn't catch him.
And-- but-- but you were angry.
So that's-- OK, he was hungrier than us.
But we needed this, too.
Yes.
My father died early.
He got the typhoid fever.
He was very sick.
We couldn't get a doctor or help in the house.
But we have known this doctor in the Jewish hospital that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] outside the ghetto, in the other part of the ghetto.
And just through this protection, my father could get into the hospital.
So I was with him.
We-- we got this [NON-ENGLISH].
That was a horse-drawn carriage.
And he was already had very high fever.
He-- he was unconscious already.
And I drove him to the hospital, but I learned that two days later, he died peacefully in bed.
We had the burial, so at least he was buried like a-- like a human being.
And that was one of the casualties of typhoid fever.
My mother was alive.
My mother was casualty of the selection.
Selection-- I don't know.
Maybe I should explain what selection is.
OK.
So that was-- in the beginning, every few weeks, people were taken from the block of the houses, congregated on the streets, and standing in big-- in tight group.

And that was commission of German judges, German [? mavens, ?] who is allowed to live and who might qualify to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whom they might use for their work, for their industry, whatever.

So these people who would march in front of these people, and some were directed to the right, which meant that-- you know, judging from the people, they were young and strong-looking, healthy-looking.
And older people, or sickly-looking, or children, were sent to the left.
We didn't know, at first, what that meant.
But we knew that probably being with young and strong--
Yes.
Yes.
So shall I start from the beginning what selection was?
So there was every few weeks, people were gathered from the blocks of houses on the street.
And that was commissioner of Germans which was deciding who is able to work, who they can have any use, and the rest were sent to the other side.
So of course, to the right, there were able-looking people-- young, healthy.
And everybody else was going to the other side.
We didn't know what it meant, in the beginning.
They told us that the other people, older and the children, they would be sent to the-- to the countryside, to other places, because there-- there is not enough room in Warsaw.
There are places where they can be used for perhaps other work.
So we didn't worry too much.
And one-- one of the selections, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sent to the right side.
So was my sister.
And my mother went to the other side.
And there was no way.
We wanted somehow to be together, but that was already like a wall between us and a lot of people.
We lost her.
And-- But we still were optimistic, and Henry assured us that she will have work there, that she will survive, she would be safe.
And we believed.
We still believed in this.
And that was very comforting at that time.
And we were glad, at least, that she had a chance.
Later, we did find out to the contrary, that all these people were sent immediately-- there were a few death camps around Warsaw.
Treblinka was the closest one.
And all my members of my family were sent to Treblinka for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for immediate, immediate death.
Of course, we didn't get any-- any information anywhere about these people.

So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with all these atrocities, the fact that we-- we-- we didn't know the truth helped us survive our own life, and trying to-- to protect ourself, to be able to be with the families, together.
Did you believe--
After my mother left, I was devastated for a couple days.
It-- it was this instinctive feeling, even that I was reassured that she will live, she will survive.
But the fact that we were separated, it was very hard to-- to accept.
I remember, I was staying in bed for couple days.
I didn't want to eat or do anything.
But again, the life comes back to you.
It's a-- it's a strong instinct with-- with young people.
So I guess we got back into some activities.
I don't remember if we worked at that time, or-- or taking these courses.
But anyway, we started to be active again.
And then, of course, this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life was deteriorating.
There were more often selections, and-- and at, unexpectedly, at different time of the days.
And there were more people coming to the ghetto.
There were some taken to the concentration camps.
And was tremendous influx of-- of people from-- from other towns or other countries.
Rather, rather from Poland.
Mostly from Poland and from Germany.
So that was this constant movement of people.
There were some disposed of, and here you have a wave of new ones again.
And just to-- to-- to live in this atmosphere, it's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was hard to maintain-- this what you were.
This what you were trained as a child to be.
But again, that was hope and survival.
So that-- that narrowed down to-- to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what-- what you were fighting for.
Yes.
After my mother left, we lived in the same house, yes, with my sister and us.
And of course, we have to adjust somehow and go about our activities.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I am trying to remember.
After-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yes.
She-- she worked for the factory.
They were German industrialists.
That's was Schultz and Toebbens.
They were either sewing factories or other manufacturing.
And she worked for the-- for the Toebbens factory.
And I know she had two friends.
They were single girls, only left out of the whole families.
And they became close.
So I think they lived together.
I mean, later, they-- they moved, and they worked together.
So-- and we again, were doing all different, like every day, different job, going to different workshop, and just living, spending one day after another.
But we began to realize the reality of the situation.
People were coming back from concentration camps with the news.
At first, we didn't want to believe it.
It was impossible.
We thought that they were crazy, that was maybe conspiracy to create a panic, to-- to-- to have people commit suicide or whatever.
But the more and more news, and it sounded very real.
So we began to think, what could be done?
That it cannot-- that-- that there is no future, and the persecution and the living conditions were completely-- they were impossible.
They were very hard to-- to tolerate.
So [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] there were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not too many, not too much, that we could do.
We were already at the point of breaking, or breaking down.
Were you thinking of escape?
Not at that point yet.
That came shortly afterwards.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was one day that we came back to the ghetto, after working all day in this Luftwaffe, which was one of the-- the best labors.
We-- we came home, and in the evening, unexpectedly, that was the selection, the action.
Selection of people, whoever was on the street.
Probably there was some more room left in the cattle cars which were sending to Treblinka.
So they needed 100, 150 people.
So whoever was on the street, we were gathered and sent straight running to the Umschlagplatz, to the station.
There were the cars prepared with big openings.
And we were on the way there.
And that time, Henry was desperate already, and he started to beg this German soldier, at least save my wife.
She wants to live.
Save my life.
And this SS man, he got angry.
He hit him on the neck, which he was suffering for the rest of his life.
He-- And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but we continued to go.
I was already almost on the platform going to the train, and this young German soldier that worked on the Luftwaffe, he knew Henry.
And he said, stop, halt.
We need this man.
And he pulled him back, and of course, he pulled him-- me with him together.
There was another man that worked, also, he was safe.
But his wife was already on the train.
She started screaming and yelling, and begging, help me!
But the doors were already shut off, and the train started to roll.
So that was one of the strong chapters.
And somehow this young German, he was humane enough.
He took us, Henry and me, on the-- his motorcycle and drove us back to the ghetto.
So that was already strong signal.
And we were spared this time.
